buggy_code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

returns = pd.read_csv('strategy_returns.csv', index_col='date', parse_dates=True)['returns']

# Wrong: cumsum of returns is an approximation that diverges significantly
equity_curve = (1 + returns).cumsum()

plt.plot(equity_curve)
plt.title('Equity Curve')
plt.show()
clean_code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

returns = pd.read_csv('strategy_returns.csv', index_col='date', parse_dates=True)['returns']

# Correct: cumprod for compound returns
equity_curve = (1 + returns).cumprod()

plt.plot(equity_curve)
plt.title('Equity Curve')
plt.show()
description: cumsum() used instead of cumprod() for computing equity curve from returns.
explanation: cumsum() of (1+r) computes the arithmetic sum, not the geometric compound. Over time, this diverges significantly from actual portfolio value. A 10% gain followed by 10% loss is (1.10 * 0.90 = 0.99) with cumprod — a 1% loss. With cumsum, it appears as (1.10 + 0.90 = 2.0) which is meaningless. Performance metrics derived from this curve will be completely wrong.
suggested_fix: Use (1 + returns).cumprod() to compute the correct compound equity curve.

issues.title: cumsum used instead of cumprod for compound equity curve
